subsections.title: Key Differences from Individual Business
bullets: Free cover limit: group schemes may have a free cover limit below which members are not individually underwritten, reducing anti-selection risk.

Control of the intermediary: an intermediary may influence available data; the insurer must ensure information is accurate and consistent with competitors. The insurer may also want to influence group policyholders to reduce work-related health risks and encourage return to work, but this may be hindered by the intermediary.

Limited insured information: full details of individual members may not be known at the start of the insurance period. Changes in staff also occur during cover. A deposit premium is paid upfront, followed by a retrospective adjustment premium at period end.

Changes in the workforce can significantly alter the riskiness of a group: leavers may be healthy or unhealthy depending on reason for leaving; joiners are typically healthy (especially if young); downsizing may prompt members to claim while still covered; workforce expansion from a merger/acquisition requires careful review.
Flexible benefits (cafeteria plans) increase anti-selection risk by permitting individuals to choose covers and amounts. The insurer may impose limits and restrictions on benefit choices.

key_terms.definition: The level of benefit below which group members are automatically accepted without individual underwriting.
An upfront premium paid at the start of the group insurance period, subject to retrospective adjustment at period end based on actual membership.
A scheme structure allowing employees to choose from a menu of benefit options, increasing anti-selection risk for the insurer.

exam_qa.q: Why do changes in the workforce affect claims experience under a group scheme?
exam_qa.a: Leavers may be healthy or unhealthy depending on their reason for leaving. Joiners tend to be young and healthy. Downsizing may encourage remaining members to claim while still covered. Merger-driven growth may improve experience initially but needs careful monitoring.

exam_qa.q: Why does the insurer need control over the intermediary in group business?
exam_qa.a: The intermediary influences the data available to the insurer and may prevent the insurer from directly encouraging policyholders to reduce health risks or return to work, both of which affect claims experience.

subsections.title: Standard Rating Approach
bullets: The usual procedures apply: judge data for relevance to future experience; subdivide into homogeneous risk cells; adjust to make appropriate for future experience (both claim incidence and claim size, e.g. for inflation).
Rating factors for group business include individual-level factors (age, gender, occupation) and group-level factors (industry, location).
The book rate is the insurer's standard premium for a group of given characteristics, before any adjustment for that group's own past experience.

key_terms.definition: The insurer's standard premium rate for a group based on general rating factors, before experience rating adjustment.

subsections.title: The Credibility Formula
bullets: The renewal premium under experience rating is: RP = Z × A + (1 − Z) × E + L, where E is the book premium, A is the risk premium based on the group's own past experience, Z is the credibility factor (0 ≤ Z ≤ 1), and L is an expense/profit loading.
The credibility factor Z depends on the volume and reliability of the group's past experience data. Higher Z means more weight on the group's own experience.
Premiums for which Z > 0 are experience rated.

Experience rating is used because a book rate may be inappropriate due to peculiarities of a specific workplace, working processes, or the employer's attitude to safety. The group's own claims history is often the best predictor of future claims.
Experience rating makes premiums more competitive and reduces the need for a large number of rating factors. It is unsuitable for new or very small groups with insufficient past data.

key_terms.definition: A system where the premium for a group contract depends wholly or partly on the group's own past claims experience.
The weight given to the group's own experience in the premium formula, ranging from 0 (full book rate) to 1 (full experience rate).
The estimated cost of claims in the forthcoming period, calculated from past experience adjusted for changes in membership, cover and medical inflation.

exam_qa.q: Why is experience rating used for most group health contracts?
exam_qa.a: A single book rate may be inappropriate due to the peculiarities of a specific workplace, working processes, or employer safety attitudes. The group's own past claims history, suitably adjusted, is often the best predictor of future claims. It also makes premiums more competitive and reduces the number of rating factors needed.

exam_qa.q: When is experience rating unsuitable?
exam_qa.a: When the group's past claims experience is insufficient or unreliable, for example for a new or very small group.

subsections.title: Burning Cost Method
bullets: The burning cost is the estimated cost of claims in the coming period, calculated from past years' experience adjusted for: changes in the numbers insured, changes in the nature of cover, and medical inflation.
Yearly burning cost calculations compare emerging claim costs against risk premiums paid, providing the earliest possible feedback on premium adequacy.
Large individual claims are typically stripped out of the base data and added back using a grossing-up factor based on the ratio of large to total claims across all similar group policies.
Claims data must be adjusted to a full year (e.g. if only 9 months of data are available for the most recent year) and inflated to the midpoint of the prospective cover period.
Trend analysis is performed on the per-employee cost to identify and project any underlying deterioration or improvement.

exam_qa.q: How are large claims handled in a burning cost calculation?
exam_qa.a: Large claims are stripped out of the base claims data. A grossing-up factor (based on the ratio of large to total claims across all similar group policies) is then applied to add back an allowance for large claims, smoothing the distortion caused by one-off large events.

subsections.title: Group PMI Pricing Features
bullets: Experience rating is used extensively for group PMI pricing, because group PMI experience differs significantly between employers even in the same industry.

Reasons experience varies between groups: different geographical locations affect private medical treatment costs; nature of work affects employees' medical needs; employer attitudes to health and safety differ.
NCD systems may be used alongside experience rating to incentivise good claims behaviour.
Larger groups are often flat rated according to benefit class (e.g. all members of the same plan tier pay the same rate). The largest groups may self-insure.
Experience rating for group PMI encourages employers to actively manage claims and promote workplace health, reducing overall costs.
Applying group-specific premiums improves marketability and sales volume by making pricing fairer for low-claims groups.

key_terms.definition: Charging the same premium rate to all members within a benefit class, typically used for larger group PMI schemes.
Where the largest employers bear claims risk themselves rather than transferring it to an insurer, often with reinsurance above a certain threshold.

exam_qa.q: Why is group PMI experience rated rather than book rated?
exam_qa.a: Group PMI experience varies significantly between employers even in the same industry, due to differences in location (affecting treatment costs), work practices (affecting health needs), and employer attitudes to health and safety. Past claims history is a powerful predictor of future experience, and experience rating encourages employers to reduce claims costs.

subsections.title: Overview of Profit Criteria
bullets: A profit criterion is a single figure that tries to summarise the profit signature of a contract. The profit signature is the sequence of profits from inception to termination — it is the definitive guide to profitability but unwieldy for comparison.
Three main profit criteria: Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), and Discounted Payback Period (DPP).
Sensitivity tests should be performed throughout the pricing process to ensure different future scenarios do not lead to serious financial difficulty.

key_terms.definition: The sequence of expected annual profits from policy inception to termination, used as the definitive measure of a product's profitability.
A single figure derived from the profit signature (e.g. NPV, IRR, DPP) used to compare the profitability of different product designs.

subsections.title: Net Present Value (NPV)
bullets: NPV is calculated by discounting the profit signature at the risk discount rate. A positive NPV means the rate of return exceeds the risk discount rate target.
Economic theory states NPV is the best profit criterion: an investor should choose the investment with the higher NPV to maximise company value.
NPV is subject to the law of diminishing returns — a company cannot sell unlimited policies with positive NPV as sales costs rise as the market saturates.
NPV is typically expressed as a percentage of initial commission (reflecting sales effort) or as a percentage of the present value of future premiums (reflecting market share).
NPV says nothing about competition — there is no point designing a high-NPV contract that cannot be sold.

key_terms.definition: The discounted value of the profit signature at the risk discount rate. The theoretically optimal profit criterion.

exam_qa.q: How is NPV expressed to make it useful for comparing products?
exam_qa.a: Either as a percentage of initial commission (measuring profit relative to sales effort) or as a percentage of the present value of future premiums (measuring profit relative to market share). A positive NPV indicates the product meets the risk discount rate target.

exam_qa.q: What are the drawbacks of NPV as a profit criterion?
exam_qa.a: It is subject to the law of diminishing returns; it says nothing about competition; comparisons are only valid if capital markets are perfectly free and efficient and the risk discount rate correctly reflects the riskiness of each investment.

subsections.title: Internal Rate of Return (IRR) and Discounted Payback Period (DPP)
bullets: IRR is the rate at which the discounted value of cashflows is zero. A higher IRR is generally preferred, but IRR does not always agree with NPV.

Disadvantages of IRR vs NPV: may not be unique if the profit signature changes sign more than once; cannot be related to sales effort or market share; may not exist if profits are made from the outset.
IRR is easier to understand conceptually but is not more informative than NPV in most cases.
DPP is the duration at which cumulative discounted profits equal zero — the time to recover the initial capital investment at the risk discount rate.
DPP ignores all cashflows after the payback period, so will not agree with NPV. It is useful for capital-constrained companies.
Common approach: use NPV (expressed as % of initial sales costs) as the primary criterion, with DPP as a secondary reference. DPP is more useful for product design than pricing.

key_terms.definition: The discount rate at which the NPV of the profit signature equals zero. Higher IRR is preferred, but has disadvantages vs NPV.
The policy duration at which the accumulated discounted profits equal zero. Useful for capital-constrained companies but ignores later cashflows.

exam_qa.q: Why might IRR not be a unique value?
exam_qa.a: If the profit signature changes sign more than once (i.e. profits alternate between positive and negative), there may be multiple discount rates at which NPV equals zero, so IRR is not unique.

subsections.title: Marketability
bullets: Premiums produced by the pricing model must be assessed for marketability. This may lead to reconsideration of: the product design; the distribution channel; the company's profit requirement; whether to proceed at all.
Re-examination of assumed expenses is also required, notably acquisition costs (commission), marginal administration costs, and contribution to fixed overheads.

subsections.title: Competitiveness
bullets: A close watch must be kept on competitors' pricing. Significant discrepancies should be investigated, though differences in target markets may explain them.
If a competitor uses more detailed risk differentiation, better risks migrate to the competitor — the insurer’s averaged pricing assumptions are called into question (anti-selection).
Two factors: (1) overall premium level vs competitors; (2) number and accuracy of rating factors used.
Fixed expenses are spread over volume — lower-than-expected volume increases per-policy expense loadings and reduces competitiveness (economy of scale principle).
Loss leaders: products priced below the full premium to gain market share, compensated by higher margins on other lines. Risk: adverse business mix if loss-leader sells more than high-margin lines.
Over time, each product should be brought back in line to produce its fair contribution to profits.
Return on capital: each profit centre must meet its return on capital target. Excess return suggests pricing can be relaxed to promote volume; insufficient return is grounds for withdrawal.

key_terms.definition: A product priced below its full expected cost to gain market share, with the shortfall compensated by margins on other product lines.
Any collection of business items with a separate target for contribution to insurer profit, ranging from a broad business line to a very specific segment.

exam_qa.q: What are the two factors involved in the competitive impact on pricing?
exam_qa.a: (1) The overall level of premiums compared with competitors; (2) the number and accuracy of rating factors used. Using fewer rating factors than competitors creates anti-selection as better risks go elsewhere.

sections.title: Reserving, Solvency Capital and Reinsurance Impact

subsections.title: Cost of Reserving and Solvency Capital
bullets: Writing a policy creates a need for regulatory reserves and solvency capital. Each policy should be charged for the cost of this capital.

The charge is the investment cost of holding assets as locked-in statutory capital rather than deploying them freely. Simplistically: if IRR is 10% and statutory assets yield 4%, the cost is 6% p.a. of the solvency capital requirement.

Reserving assumptions in the pricing model should match those the insurer realistically expects to use (prudent if regulations require prudent reserves; best estimate if regulations require best estimate reserves).
Solvency capital requirements may be reduced by suitable reinsurance — reinsurers may hold less capital per unit of risk, with cost savings potentially passed to consumers.

key_terms.definition: The charge in pricing for the investment return foregone by holding assets as locked-in statutory capital rather than deploying them freely.

exam_qa.q: Should reserving assumptions in the pricing model be best estimate or prudent?
exam_qa.a: They should match the assumptions the insurer realistically expects to use in practice under regulation — prudent if the regulatory regime requires prudent reserves, best estimate if the regime requires best estimate reserves. The pricing model must also allow for any solvency capital requirements on top of reserves.

subsections.title: Reinsurance Impact on Pricing
bullets: Reinsurance roles in pricing: technical assistance/data for new products; risk sharing; smoothing profitability; financing new business strain; tax arbitrage; solvency capital arbitrage; enabling acceptance of larger risks.
Unless arbitrage is possible, the insurer will expect a net loss from reinsurance (premiums paid > expected recoveries), which must be included in the pricing model.
Where financial benefits of reinsurance exceed costs (e.g. tax arbitrage), pricing net of reinsurance can improve competitiveness.
Under quota share arrangements, the reinsurer may require the right to vary commission if the insurer changes its premium rates.

exam_qa.q: Why does reinsurance usually have a net cost to the insurer?
exam_qa.a: The reinsurer must cover its own expenses and profit, so reinsurance premiums will generally exceed the expected present value of reinsurance benefits. This net cost must be included in the pricing model unless some arbitrage (e.g. tax or capital) makes the arrangement financially beneficial.

subsections.title: Regulatory Impact on Pricing
bullets: Pricing basis may be prescribed by the regulator (e.g. community rating) or certain rating factors restricted (e.g. gender, genetic test results).
Regulatory restrictions on operations (e.g. investment constraints, requirement to collect premium taxes) impose costs that must be reflected in pricing assumptions.
Such additions to costs affect all insurers equally and so do not alter competitive positions.

key_terms.definition: A regulatory requirement to charge all policyholders the same premium regardless of individual risk characteristics.

subsections.title: Monitoring and Reviewing Premium Rates
bullets: All items in the assumption set must be kept under review. When any assumption is no longer valid, its impact must be tested against the existing tariff before deciding whether to reprice. Changing prices incurs expense and may affect marketability.
For reviewable premium business: the actuary estimates premiums subject to periodic review. Margins are smaller because adverse experience can be corrected at the next review.
Risk of selective lapsing on rate increase: healthier lives are more likely to lapse if premiums rise significantly, leaving a worse risk pool. The rate increase must account for this risk.

In some territories, regulators specify what aspects of the basis can justify a premium review (e.g. in the UK, premiums can only increase for 'valid reason' related to general portfolio experience, not individual experience).
Where premiums are inadequate (e.g. loss leaders), reserves must be established. Their size depends on the speed of premium review and the likelihood of renewal as rates rise.

key_terms.definition: The tendency for healthier lives to lapse when premiums are increased, leaving the remaining pool with worse-than-average risk.

exam_qa.q: Why do reviewable premium products include smaller margins than guaranteed premium products?
exam_qa.a: Because any significant deterioration in future experience can be corrected at the next premium review. Guaranteed premium products cannot be repriced, so they need much larger margins to cover possible adverse experience over the full policy term.

exam_qa.q: Why must actuaries be careful not to increase reviewable premiums by too much?
exam_qa.a: A large increase encourages selective lapsing — healthier lives leave, leaving a worse risk pool. This can worsen the insurer's position, as the per-policy expense cost also rises on a smaller portfolio, potentially negating the benefit of the rate increase.

subsections.title: Nature and Terms of Health Options
bullets: Mortality/morbidity options (health options) allow policyholders to extend term or increase cover at normal premium rates without providing further medical evidence.

Common examples: option to purchase additional cover without evidence of health at normal rates; option to renew a CI policy at term end without further evidence; buy-back option to reinstate mortality cover after an accelerated CI claim.
Terms and conditions are designed to reduce anti-selection risk. Options may only be exercisable at fixed points (e.g. every 5 years) or on qualifying life events (e.g. marriage, new job).
For IP options, the policyholder must not be currently claiming or within a deferred/linked-claim period when exercising the option. Someone who has recovered may be eligible again.
Unlike life options (exercisable provided alive), health options cannot be exercised while sick — but are still valuable to those who have deteriorating health but are not yet claiming.

key_terms.definition: A policy feature allowing the policyholder to extend or increase cover at standard rates without further medical underwriting.
An option to reinstate the mortality cover under an accelerated CI policy after a CI claim has been paid, without fresh underwriting.

exam_qa.q: Why are health options valuable even if they cannot be exercised while the policyholder is sick?
exam_qa.a: There is a significant category of policyholders who are not yet sick enough to claim but have already developed conditions that increase their future risk (e.g. hypertension). These lives benefit most from exercising options at standard rates, since they would face rated or declined terms if underwritten afresh.

subsections.title: The Cost of Options
bullets: The cost of an option is the value of the excess of the premium that should be charged (given full underwriting information) over the normal premium rate that is actually charged.
The total cost depends on: (1) the health status of those who exercise the option; (2) the proportion of eligible lives who choose to exercise it.
In general, the smaller the proportion exercising the option, the worse the average health of those who do exercise it.
Cost of option ≈ proportion of lives exercising option × average extent that their health is worse than select.
If all eligible policyholders exercise the option (including healthy ones), the insurer is better off because healthy lives pay appropriate premiums, generating additional profit.
The value of options must be kept under constant review — a widespread disease outbreak could cause option costs to far exceed original pricing assumptions.

exam_qa.q: Is the insurer better off if all policyholders exercise the option, or only the unhealthy ones?
exam_qa.a: Better off if all policyholders exercise it. Healthy policyholders pay premiums appropriate to their select morbidity, generating additional profits. If only unhealthy lives exercise the option, the insurer bears the full anti-selection cost with no offsetting income from healthy lives.

subsections.title: Valuation Method and Assumptions
bullets: Options are normally valued using cashflow projections including: additional benefits expected under the option; additional premiums received (based on standard rates for a select life at the option date); any additional expenses (e.g. reminder letters, processing costs).
For pricing purposes, the valuation must also allow for additional reserves held before and after exercise.

Key assumptions required: probability that the option is exercised at each possible date; additional benefit level chosen; expected morbidity of lives who exercise vs those who do not; additional expenses.
Conventional method: assume all eligible policyholders take up the option at the maximum additional benefit. Simple but unrealistic; likely to overstate cost.
North American method: use more realistic take-up rate assumptions varying by exercise date and alternative option. More accurate but requires data (historically available only in North America).

Typically, the expected morbidity of lives who take up the option is heavier than those who do not (due to anti-selection). This can be expressed as a percentage loading on the base table (e.g. 150%) or an age addition (e.g. age x experiences mortality of age x+5).

A consistent set of assumptions is needed: the average mortality of all lives (exercisers and non-exercisers) should remain at the base expected level. If exercisers are assumed heavier, non-exercisers must be assumed lighter to maintain the average.
Stochastic modelling can be used to establish the core cost of options, simulating the distribution of take-up rates and subsequent morbidity across many scenarios.

key_terms.definition: Values options by assuming all eligible policyholders take up the option at the maximum benefit. Simple but tends to overstate cost.
Values options using realistic take-up rate assumptions based on observed experience. More accurate but requires credible data.

exam_qa.q: Why does a policy with a morbidity option need higher reserves than the equivalent policy without?
exam_qa.a: The option increases morbidity risk — an option to extend term increases the exposure period, an option to increase sum insured increases the amount at risk. Reserves must increase for expected additional claims and for the uncertainty in those claims. Before exercise, the insurer faces anti-selection risk; after exercise, it holds lives of unknown health without underwriting evidence.

exam_qa.q: Why must the morbidity assumption for non-exercisers be set lower than select if exercisers are assumed heavier?
exam_qa.a: The average morbidity across all lives (exercisers and non-exercisers) must equal the base expected level. If exercisers are assumed heavier (e.g. ultimate + 10 years), non-exercisers must be lighter than ultimate to maintain the average. Failing to do this would implicitly assume the overall portfolio is worse than expected.

subsections.title: Pricing and Reserving for Guarantees
bullets: Guaranteed premium rates must be assessed on a resilience basis: will the premium be sufficient to meet all outgoings under a range of adverse scenarios, with an agreed level of confidence?
The actuary must incorporate into the premium a loading for the additional cost of providing the guarantee, including the cost of holding guarantee reserves.
If the true cost of guarantees is reflected in premiums, it may encourage policyholders to prefer reviewable contracts (same benefits, lower premium because no guarantee cost).

Conventional (without-profits) contracts transfer mortality/morbidity, expense and investment risk from policyholder to insurer. Health and care contracts rarely include financially onerous investment guarantees because reserves are small relative to benefit amounts.
Exception: pre-funded LTCI, where large reserves accumulate and investment risk is material if the contract is written on fully guaranteed terms.
The local regulator will normally want to review the techniques and outputs used to justify the adequacy of guaranteed premiums and reserves.

key_terms.definition: Assessment of whether premium rates remain sufficient under a range of adverse scenarios, required when pricing guaranteed contracts.
An additional reserve held to cover the expected cost of providing a premium or benefit guarantee under a long-term contract.

exam_qa.q: Why do most health and care contracts not pose onerous investment guarantees on insurers?
exam_qa.a: Most health and care products are protection rather than savings contracts. Policy reserves are usually small relative to benefit amounts, so even significant underperformance of investment returns has little financial impact on the insurer. The main risk is claim incidence, not investment returns.

exam_qa.q: What is the risk of fully reflecting the guarantee cost in the premium?
exam_qa.a: It may make the guaranteed product significantly more expensive than an equivalent reviewable product. Policyholders may then opt for the reviewable contract, undermining sales volumes for the guaranteed product.
